CHAPTER 6 · CLASS 10 · BIOLOGY

Control & Coordination

Nervous System · Reflex Arc · Human Brain · Plant Hormones · Endocrine System

⚡ Nervous System 🧠 Human Brain 🔁 Reflex Arc 🌿 Plant Tropisms 🧪 Hormones 🔬 Endocrine Glands
⚡NEURONS
🧠BRAIN
🔁REFLEX
🌿TROPISM
🧪HORMONES

Living organisms detect changes in their environment and respond with controlled, coordinated movements

⚡ Neurons transmit information as electrical impulses 🔁 Reflex arc is formed in the spinal cord for quick responses 🧠 Cerebellum maintains posture and balance 🌿 Auxin causes shoots to bend towards light (phototropism) 🧪 Adrenaline — the "fight or flight" hormone from adrenal glands 🔬 Insulin from pancreas regulates blood sugar levels ⚡ Neurons transmit information as electrical impulses 🔁 Reflex arc is formed in the spinal cord for quick responses 🧠 Cerebellum maintains posture and balance 🌿 Auxin causes shoots to bend towards light (phototropism) 🧪 Adrenaline — the "fight or flight" hormone from adrenal glands 🔬 Insulin from pancreas regulates blood sugar levels
01 · INTRODUCTION

What is Control & Coordination?

Living organisms respond to changes in their environment (stimuli) with controlled, purposeful movements. Such controlled movement must be connected to the recognition of events, followed by the correct response. This requires a system of control and coordination.

⚡

Nervous System (Animals)

Uses electrical impulses via nerve cells (neurons) for rapid, targeted communication. Fast but limited to cells connected by nervous tissue.

🌿

Chemical Coordination (Plants)

Plants use hormones (chemical signals) that diffuse to cells. No nervous system or muscles — movement by changing water content in cells.

🧪

Hormonal System (Animals)

Uses hormones secreted into the blood. Slower but reaches all cells. Provides long-term, body-wide coordination (e.g. growth, metabolism).

02 · NERVOUS SYSTEM

6.1 Animals — Nervous System

All information from the environment is detected by receptors — specialised tips of nerve cells located in sense organs (inner ear, nose, tongue, eyes, skin). Gustatory receptors detect taste; olfactory receptors detect smell.

How a Nerve Impulse Travels

🌿
Dendrite tip (Receptor)

Information acquired at the dendritic tip of a nerve cell. A chemical reaction creates an electrical impulse.

↓
🔵
Cell Body

Impulse travels from dendrite → cell body (contains nucleus and cytoplasm).

↓
➡️
Axon

Impulse travels along the axon to its end. Axon is a long fibre — the "wire" of the neuron.

↓
⚗️
Synapse — Chemical Signal

At the axon end, electrical impulse triggers release of chemicals. These cross the synapse (gap between neurons) and start a new impulse in the next neuron's dendrite.

↓
💪
Effector (Muscle / Gland)

Finally, impulse is delivered from neurons to muscle cells or glands via a similar synapse (neuromuscular junction). Action is performed.

Fig 6.1 — Structure of Neuron & Neuromuscular Junction

Fig 6.1 — (a) Structure of neuron, (b) Neuromuscular junction (NCERT)

🔬 Nervous tissue is made up of an organised network of neurons, specialised for conducting information via electrical impulses from one part of the body to another.

📌 PARTS OF A NEURON

Dendrite — receives information (input)
Cell body (Cyton) — contains nucleus; processes information
Axon — carries impulse away from cell body (output)
Nerve ending — releases chemical at synapse
Myelin sheath — insulates axon; speeds up conduction

🧪 ACTIVITY 6.1 — Taste and Smell

  • Put some sugar in your mouth. Note the taste.
  • Block your nose by pressing it. Eat sugar again — is there any difference?
  • Block your nose while eating lunch — can you fully taste the food?

Why does blocking the nose affect taste? Gustatory (taste) and olfactory (smell) receptors both contribute to what we perceive as "taste". With the nose blocked, olfactory input is cut off. This is why food tastes different when you have a cold.

IN-TEXT QUESTIONS

  1. What is the difference between a reflex action and walking?
  2. What happens at the synapse between two neurons?
  3. Which part of the brain maintains posture and equilibrium of the body?
  4. How do we detect the smell of an agarbatti (incense stick)?
  5. What is the role of the brain in reflex action?
03 · REFLEX ACTIONS

6.1.1 The Reflex Arc

A reflex action is a sudden, automatic response to a stimulus — done without thinking. Examples: pulling hand from flame, pupil size change, mouth watering. They evolved because the brain is not fast enough for emergency responses.

🔁 How a Reflex Arc Works

1
Stimulus detected

Receptors in skin (e.g. heat/pain receptors) detect the stimulus.

2
Sensory neuron carries impulse

Impulse travels via sensory (afferent) neuron to the spinal cord.

3
Relay neuron in spinal cord

A relay (interneuron) in the spinal cord connects sensory to motor neuron. Impulse also goes to the brain (we feel pain after the reflex).

4
Motor neuron carries response

Impulse travels via motor (efferent) neuron back to the effector.

5
Effector responds

Muscle in arm contracts — hand is pulled away. This all happens before you consciously feel pain.

📌 Reflex arcs are formed in the spinal cord (not the brain). This makes responses extremely fast. The brain receives the information simultaneously but the action is already done.

Fig 6.2 — Reflex Arc

Fig 6.2 — Reflex arc showing receptor → sensory neuron → relay neuron (spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector (NCERT)

⚡ REFLEX ARC COMPONENTS

Receptor — detects stimulus (e.g. skin receptors)
Sensory neuron — carries impulse to CNS
Relay neuron — connects sensory to motor (in spinal cord)
Motor neuron — carries response to effector
Effector — muscle or gland that responds

CNS = Brain + Spinal cord = Central Nervous System
PNS = Cranial nerves + Spinal nerves = Peripheral Nervous System

💡 Voluntary vs Involuntary: Reflex actions are involuntary (not under conscious control). Walking is voluntary — involves conscious thought and the brain directs muscle movement.

04 · HUMAN BRAIN

6.1.2 The Human Brain

The brain is the main coordinating centre. The brain + spinal cord = Central Nervous System (CNS). The brain receives signals from all over the body, processes them, and sends responses. It is protected inside a bony box (cranium/skull) and surrounded by cerebrospinal fluid for shock absorption.

Fig 6.3 — Human Brain

Fig 6.3 — Human brain showing fore-brain, mid-brain, hind-brain and associated structures (NCERT)

🧠 FORE-BRAIN

Main thinking part. Has regions for receiving sensory impulses — hearing, smell, sight, etc. Separate association areas interpret information. Controls voluntary muscles. Hunger centre also here.

🔆 MID-BRAIN

Controls many involuntary actions — e.g. change in pupil size in response to light. Relays signals between fore-brain and hind-brain.

🌀 HIND-BRAIN — 3 parts

Cerebellum — precision of voluntary actions, posture and balance of the body (walking, riding, picking up a pencil).
Medulla — controls all involuntary actions: blood pressure, salivation, vomiting, heart rate, breathing.
Pons — bridges cerebellum and medulla; involved in breathing regulation.

🛡️ HOW TISSUES ARE PROTECTED

Brain: Enclosed in a bony box (cranium/skull) + cerebrospinal fluid in a fluid-filled balloon for shock absorption.
Spinal cord: Protected by the vertebral column (backbone) — the hard bumpy structure you feel down the middle of your back.

05 · COORDINATION IN PLANTS

6.2 Coordination in Plants

Plants have no nervous system and no muscles. They respond to stimuli using chemical means (hormones) and by changing water content in cells. Plants show two types of movement:

⚡ 6.2.1 Immediate Response (Not growth-related)

Example: Mimosa pudica (touch-me-not / chhui-mui). Leaves fold rapidly on touch — this is NOT due to growth.

Fig 6.4 — The Sensitive Plant (Mimosa pudica)

Fig 6.4 — Mimosa pudica before and after touch (NCERT)

1
Touch detected

Information is communicated from point of touch to other cells via electrical-chemical means (no specialised tissue).

2
Cells change shape

Plant cells change their water content — swelling or shrinking — which changes their shape, causing the leaves to fold/droop.

🌱 6.2.2 Movement Due to Growth (Tropisms)

Tropic movements — directional growth in response to a stimulus. Can be towards (positive) or away from (negative) the stimulus.

TropismStimulusResponse
PhototropismLightShoot bends towards light (+); Root bends away (−)
GeotropismGravityRoot grows downward (+); Shoot grows upward (−)
HydrotropismWaterRoot grows towards water (+)
ChemotropismChemicalPollen tube grows towards ovule (+)
ThigmotropismTouch/ContactTendrils coil around support

Fig 6.5 — Phototropism

Fig 6.5 — Response of plant to direction of light

Fig 6.6 — Geotropism

Fig 6.6 — Plant showing geotropism

Plant Hormones — Chemical Coordination

🌱

Auxin

Synthesised at shoot tip. Causes cells to grow longer. In light, diffuses to shady side → that side grows longer → shoot bends towards light. Causes phototropism and thigmotropism (tendril coiling).

🌿

Gibberellins

Like auxins, help in growth of the stem (stem elongation). Also involved in seed germination and flowering.

🌾

Cytokinins

Promote cell division. Present in greater concentration in areas of rapid division — fruits and seeds.

🍂

Abscisic Acid (ABA)

Growth inhibitor. Inhibits growth. Its effects include wilting of leaves. Acts as a stress hormone (e.g. during drought).

🌿 ACTIVITY 6.2 — Phototropism in Bean Seeds

  • Fill a conical flask with water, cover neck with wire mesh, keep germinated bean seeds on mesh.
  • Place in an open box facing a window (light from one side) for 2–3 days.
  • Observe: shoots bend towards light, roots away from light.
  • Now turn the flask so shoots face away from light. Observe old and new growth directions.

Old parts do not change direction, but new growth shows the new tropism direction — proving it is growth-dependent.

IN-TEXT QUESTIONS

  1. What are plant hormones?
  2. How is the movement of leaves of the sensitive plant different from the movement of a shoot towards light?
  3. Give an example of a plant hormone that promotes growth.
  4. How do auxins promote the growth of a tendril around a support?
  5. Design an experiment to demonstrate hydrotropism.
06 · HORMONES IN ANIMALS

6.3 Hormones in Animals

Animal hormones are secreted by endocrine glands (ductless glands) directly into the blood. They are transported to target organs. The timing and amount of hormone released are controlled by a feedback mechanism.

Fig 6.7 — Endocrine Glands in Human Beings

Fig 6.7 — Endocrine glands in human beings (a) male, (b) female (NCERT)

⚡ Adrenaline — Fight or Flight

Secreted by adrenal glands (above kidneys) in scary/emergency situations. Released directly into blood.

❤️
Heart beats faster

More oxygen supplied to muscles.

🩸
Blood diverted to skeletal muscles

Blood supply reduced to digestive system and skin (muscles around small arteries contract).

🫁
Breathing rate increases

Diaphragm and rib muscles contract more rapidly.

All these responses prepare the body to fight or flee — the classic "fight or flight" response. Adrenaline is part of the endocrine system.

Table 6.1 — Important Hormones and Their Functions

#HormoneEndocrine GlandFunctions
1Growth HormonePituitary glandStimulates growth in all organs. Deficiency in childhood → dwarfism.
2ThyroxinThyroid glandRegulates carbohydrate, protein and fat metabolism for body growth. Needs iodine for synthesis. Deficiency → goitre (swollen neck).
3InsulinPancreasRegulates blood sugar levels. Deficiency → diabetes mellitus (high blood sugar). Treatment: insulin injections.
4TestosteroneTestesDevelopment of male sex organs and secondary sexual characteristics at puberty.
5OestrogenOvariesDevelopment of female sex organs, regulates menstrual cycle, secondary sexual characteristics.
6AdrenalineAdrenal glandsFight or flight response — increases heart rate, breathing rate, diverts blood to muscles.
7Releasing hormonesHypothalamusStimulates pituitary gland to release hormones (e.g. growth hormone releasing factor).

🔄 FEEDBACK MECHANISM

Hormone secretion is self-regulating via feedback. Example: when blood sugar rises, pancreas detects it and secretes more insulin. As blood sugar falls, insulin secretion is reduced. This maintains homeostasis.

💡 DO YOU KNOW? — Hypothalamus

The hypothalamus plays an important role in the release of many hormones. When growth hormone level is low, hypothalamus releases growth hormone releasing factor which stimulates the pituitary gland to release growth hormone.

IN-TEXT QUESTIONS

  1. How does chemical coordination take place in animals?
  2. Why is the use of iodised salt advisable?
  3. How does our body respond when adrenaline is secreted into the blood?
  4. Why are some patients of diabetes treated by giving injections of insulin?
07 · SUMMARY

What You Have Learnt

⚡

Nervous System

Control and coordination in animals is provided by the nervous system (electrical impulses) and hormones. Nervous system responses: reflex, voluntary, or involuntary actions.

🔁

Reflex Arc

Reflex arcs are formed in the spinal cord for quick responses. Receptor → Sensory neuron → Relay neuron (spinal cord) → Motor neuron → Effector.

🧠

Human Brain

Fore-brain (thinking), mid-brain (involuntary actions like pupil reflex), hind-brain (cerebellum for balance; medulla for involuntary actions). Protected by cranium and CSF.

🌿

Plant Coordination

Plants use hormones (chemical coordination). Two types of movement: immediate response (water content change in cells) and growth-based tropisms (phototropism, geotropism, etc.).

🧪

Animal Hormones

Hormones produced in one part move to another via blood to achieve the desired effect. A feedback mechanism regulates their action. Endocrine system = second way of control.

🔄

Feedback Mechanism

The timing and amount of hormone release is regulated by feedback mechanisms — maintaining homeostasis in the body (e.g. blood sugar regulation by insulin).

08 · KEY TERMS

Important Keywords & Definitions

TermDefinition
NeuronBasic structural and functional unit of nervous tissue (nerve cell). Consists of dendrite, cell body, axon, and nerve ending.
DendriteBranch-like projections of neuron that receive information (electrical impulses).
AxonLong fibre of the neuron that carries impulse away from the cell body to the next neuron or effector.
SynapseGap between two neurons where electrical impulse is converted to a chemical signal for transmission.
ReceptorSpecialised tips of nerve cells or sense organs that detect stimuli from the environment.
Reflex ActionSudden, automatic, involuntary response to a stimulus that does not involve conscious thought.
Reflex ArcNeural pathway for a reflex action: Receptor → Sensory neuron → Relay neuron (spinal cord) → Motor neuron → Effector.
CNSCentral Nervous System = Brain + Spinal cord. Receives and processes information from all parts of the body.
PNSPeripheral Nervous System = Cranial nerves + Spinal nerves. Connects CNS to the rest of the body.
Fore-brainMain thinking part of the brain (cerebrum + hypothalamus). Receives sensory inputs; controls voluntary actions.
CerebellumPart of hind-brain responsible for precision of voluntary actions and maintaining posture and balance.
MedullaPart of hind-brain that controls involuntary actions: blood pressure, salivation, vomiting, heart rate.
TropismDirectional growth movement of a plant in response to an external stimulus. Towards stimulus = positive; away = negative.
PhototropismGrowth movement in response to light. Shoots show positive phototropism; roots show negative phototropism.
GeotropismGrowth movement in response to gravity. Roots grow downward (+); shoots grow upward (−).
AuxinPlant hormone synthesised at shoot tip. Promotes cell elongation. Causes phototropism by accumulating on shady side.
GibberellinsPlant hormones that promote stem growth (elongation) and seed germination.
CytokininsPlant hormones that promote cell division. Found in high concentration in fruits and seeds.
Abscisic AcidPlant hormone that inhibits growth; causes wilting of leaves. Acts as a stress hormone.
HormoneChemical substance secreted by endocrine glands directly into blood, carried to target organs to produce specific effects.
Endocrine glandsDuctless glands that secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream (e.g. pituitary, thyroid, pancreas, adrenal).
AdrenalineHormone from adrenal glands; triggers fight or flight response — increases heart rate, breathing rate, diverts blood to muscles.
InsulinHormone from pancreas that regulates blood sugar level. Deficiency causes diabetes mellitus.
ThyroxinHormone from thyroid gland; regulates carbohydrate, protein and fat metabolism. Needs iodine. Deficiency → goitre.
Feedback mechanismSelf-regulating system where the output of a process controls the rate of that process (e.g. blood sugar regulates insulin secretion).
Gustatory receptorReceptor that detects taste (on tongue).
Olfactory receptorReceptor that detects smell (in nasal passage).
09 · EXERCISES

NCERT Exercises with Answers

  1. Which of the following is a plant hormone?
    (a) Insulin
    (b) Thyroxin
    (c) Oestrogen
    (d) Cytokinin
  2. The gap between two neurons is called a:
    (a) Dendrite
    (b) Synapse
    (c) Axon
    (d) Impulse
  3. The brain is responsible for:
    (a) Thinking only
    (b) Regulating the heart beat only
    (c) Balancing the body only
    (d) All of the above
  4. What is the function of receptors in our body? Think of situations where receptors do not work properly. What problems are likely to arise?
    Function of receptors: Receptors are specialised tips of sensory nerve cells (usually located in sense organs) that detect information/stimuli from the environment. They convert physical or chemical stimuli into electrical impulses that are transmitted via sensory neurons to the CNS.

    Problems if receptors don't work: (i) A person cannot detect pain — dangerous as they would not react to harmful situations (e.g. fire, injury). (ii) A blind person (non-functional eye receptors) cannot detect light/images. (iii) A deaf person (non-functional ear receptors) cannot detect sound. (iv) Loss of smell receptors → cannot detect dangerous gases or spoiled food. (v) Non-functional taste receptors → cannot taste food properly.
  5. Draw the structure of a neuron and explain its function.
    Parts and functions:
    (i) Dendrites — Branch-like projections that receive electrical impulses from the environment or from other neurons.
    (ii) Cell body (Cyton) — Contains the nucleus; integrates information; generates impulse.
    (iii) Axon — Long fibre that carries impulse away from cell body towards the next neuron or effector. May be covered by myelin sheath for faster conduction.
    (iv) Nerve ending (Axon terminal) — Releases chemical neurotransmitters at the synapse to pass impulse to the next neuron.

    Function: Neurons conduct information as electrical impulses from receptors to CNS, within CNS, and from CNS to effectors (muscles/glands). A synapse converts the electrical signal to a chemical signal for transmission between neurons.
  6. How does phototropism occur in plants?
    Phototropism is the directional growth of a plant in response to light. Mechanism: When light falls on one side of a plant, the hormone auxin (synthesised at the shoot tip) diffuses towards the shady side of the shoot. This higher concentration of auxin stimulates the cells on the shady side to grow longer than the cells on the lit side. As a result, the shoot bends towards the light (positive phototropism). Roots, on the other hand, show negative phototropism — they bend away from light.
  7. Which signals will get disrupted in case of a spinal cord injury?
    The spinal cord: (i) carries sensory signals from all parts of the body below the neck to the brain, and (ii) carries motor signals from the brain to the muscles of the body. It also (iii) forms reflex arcs for quick involuntary responses.

    In case of spinal cord injury: (i) Sensory signals from below the injury point cannot reach the brain — person loses sensation in those body parts. (ii) Motor signals from the brain cannot reach muscles below the injury — paralysis. (iii) Reflex actions mediated at that spinal cord level will be disrupted or lost.
  8. How does chemical coordination occur in plants?
    Plants coordinate their activities using plant hormones (phytohormones). These are chemical compounds synthesised in one part of the plant and transported (by diffusion) to another part where they produce an effect. Key plant hormones: Auxin (cell elongation, phototropism), Gibberellins (stem growth, germination), Cytokinins (cell division), Abscisic acid (growth inhibition, wilting). Unlike animals, plants have no specialised nervous tissue — all coordination is entirely chemical, making it slower but capable of reaching every cell.
  9. What is the need for a system of control and coordination in an organism?
    Living organisms constantly respond to changes in their environment (stimuli) to survive. Without control and coordination: (i) organisms could not detect and respond to danger (e.g. heat, predators), (ii) different organs would not work together harmoniously, (iii) growth and development would be unregulated, (iv) internal conditions (temperature, blood sugar, etc.) could not be maintained (homeostasis would fail). The nervous system provides fast responses; hormones provide slow, body-wide regulation. Together, they ensure appropriate, coordinated responses to all environmental and internal changes.
  10. How are involuntary actions and reflex actions different from each other?
    Involuntary actions are actions not under conscious control, controlled by the mid-brain and hind-brain (e.g. beating of heart, breathing, digestion, salivation). They happen continuously and are essential for life. They are not triggered by a sudden external stimulus.

    Reflex actions are also involuntary (not consciously controlled) but are sudden, rapid responses to a specific external stimulus, mediated by reflex arcs in the spinal cord (e.g. pulling hand from flame, blinking when dust enters the eye). They are quick protective responses, not continuous actions.
  11. Compare and contrast nervous and hormonal mechanisms for control and coordination in animals.
    Similarities: Both coordinate activities of the body; both involve information transmission; both are essential for homeostasis.

    Differences:
    Nervous: Electrical impulses via nerve cells; very fast; reaches only cells connected by nerves; short-lived response; specific pathway; e.g. pulling hand from flame.
    Hormonal: Chemical signals via blood; slower; reaches all body cells; long-lasting effect; no specific pathway needed; e.g. adrenaline in fight-or-flight, insulin for blood sugar, growth hormone for development.
  12. What is the difference between the manner in which movement takes place in a sensitive plant and the movement in our legs?
    Sensitive plant (Mimosa): Movement is due to change in water content of cells — cells swell or shrink causing leaves to fold/droop. No muscles or nervous system involved. Information is transmitted by electrical-chemical means without specialised tissue. Movement is not due to growth and is reversible.

    Legs (human): Movement is due to contraction of muscle cells controlled by motor neurons from the nervous system. Muscle cells have special proteins (actin and myosin) that change shape in response to nerve impulses. Voluntary movement directed by the fore-brain; involves conscious thought. Uses specialised nervous tissue and muscle tissue.
